title: Cells to Systems
subtitle: Cells, specialised cells, the microscope, and how cells build tissues, organs and systems.

studyGuide.heading: Characteristics of living things — MRS GREN
studyGuide.html:
All living organisms carry out seven life processes.

  • Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition.
studyGuide.mnemonic: MRS GREN: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition.

studyGuide.heading: Animal and plant cells
studyGuide.html:
Cells are the building blocks of all living things. Parts found in both animal and plant cells:

  • Nucleus — controls the cell and contains DNA (genetic information).
  • Cytoplasm — jelly where chemical reactions happen.
  • Cell membrane — controls what enters and leaves the cell.
  • Mitochondria — where respiration releases energy.

Found only in plant cells:

  • Cell wall (made of cellulose) — gives support and shape.
  • Chloroplasts — contain chlorophyll for photosynthesis.
  • Permanent vacuole — holds cell sap and keeps the cell firm.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.lbl{font:11px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.t{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><ellipse cx='115' cy='100' rx='95' ry='75' fill='#fde6ef' stroke='#e0524d' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='115' cy='95' r='26' fill='#5b8def'/><circle cx='115' cy='95' r='10' fill='#23467f'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='140' rx='16' ry='8' fill='#f0a35a'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='55' rx='16' ry='8' fill='#f0a35a'/><text class='t' x='115' y='192' text-anchor='middle'>Animal cell</text><rect x='250' y='35' width='180' height='130' rx='4' fill='#e6f7ef' stroke='#2b9d6b' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='300' cy='95' r='24' fill='#5b8def'/><circle cx='300' cy='95' r='9' fill='#23467f'/><ellipse cx='370' cy='70' rx='18' ry='9' fill='#2b9d6b'/><ellipse cx='360' cy='130' rx='18' ry='9' fill='#2b9d6b'/><rect x='320' y='95' width='90' height='55' rx='6' fill='#cdeafd' opacity='0.6'/><text class='t' x='340' y='192' text-anchor='middle'>Plant cell</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Plant cells have a wall, chloroplasts (green) and a large vacuole. Both have nucleus, cytoplasm, membrane.

studyGuide.heading: Using a microscope
studyGuide.html:
• Total magnification = eyepiece lens × objective lens (e.g. 10 × 40 = ×400).
  • Start with the lowest power objective to find the specimen, then increase.
  • Focus using the coarse focus first, then the fine focus.
  • Prepare a slide with a thin sample, a stain (e.g. iodine) and a cover slip lowered at an angle to avoid air bubbles.

studyGuide.heading: Specialised cells
studyGuide.html:
Cells are adapted to their job:

  • Red blood cell — no nucleus, packed with haemoglobin, biconcave shape → carries oxygen.
  • Nerve cell — long and thin → carries electrical signals quickly.
  • Sperm cell — has a tail (flagellum) → swims to the egg.
  • Root hair cell — long projection → large surface area to absorb water and minerals.
  • Muscle cell — contains many mitochondria → energy to contract.
  • Palisade cell — many chloroplasts near the leaf surface → maximises photosynthesis.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 115' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.t{font:9px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.o{fill:#fde6ef;stroke:#e0524d;stroke-width:2}.n{fill:#eaf1fe;stroke:#5b8def;stroke-width:2}.g{fill:#e6f7ef;stroke:#2b9d6b;stroke-width:2}</style><ellipse class='o' cx='52' cy='42' rx='30' ry='16'/><ellipse cx='52' cy='42' rx='12' ry='7' fill='#fff'/><text class='t' x='52' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>red blood cell</text><text class='t' x='52' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(biconcave)</text><circle class='n' cx='150' cy='42' r='15'/><line x1='150' y1='27' x2='144' y2='17' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='27' x2='156' y2='17' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='165' y1='42' x2='222' y2='42' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='3'/><text class='t' x='180' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>nerve cell</text><text class='t' x='180' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(long, thin)</text><ellipse class='g' cx='272' cy='42' rx='14' ry='10'/><path d='M286 42 q12 -10 22 0 q10 10 20 0' fill='none' stroke='#2b9d6b' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='298' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>sperm cell</text><text class='t' x='298' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(tail to swim)</text><rect class='n' x='372' y='29' width='40' height='26' rx='4'/><line x1='372' y1='42' x2='342' y2='42' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='388' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>root hair cell</text><text class='t' x='388' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(big surface)</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Each specialised cell's shape suits its job — always link the feature to the function.

studyGuide.heading: Levels of organisation
studyGuide.html:
Cells build up into bigger structures:

Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism

  • Tissue — a group of similar cells working together (e.g. muscle tissue).
  • Organ — different tissues working together (e.g. the heart, the stomach).
  • Organ system — organs working together (e.g. the digestive system).

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.b{fill:#eaf1fe;stroke:#5b8def;stroke-width:2}.t{font:bold 11px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.a{fill:#5b8def}</style><rect class='b' x='5' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='40' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Cell</text><path class='a' d='M80 47 l12 -6 v12 z'/><rect class='b' x='98' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='133' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Tissue</text><path class='a' d='M173 47 l12 -6 v12 z'/><rect class='b' x='191' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='226' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Organ</text><path class='a' d='M266 47 l12 -6 v12 z'/><rect class='b' x='284' y='26' width='80' height='42' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='324' y='44' text-anchor='middle'>Organ</text><text class='t' x='324' y='58' text-anchor='middle'>system</text><path class='a' d='M369 47 l12 -6 v12 z'/><rect class='b' x='387' y='30' width='68' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='421' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Organism</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Small to large: cell to whole organism.

studyGuide.heading: Major organ systems
studyGuide.html:
• Digestive — breaks down food (mouth, stomach, small intestine).
  • Circulatory — transports blood (heart, blood vessels).
  • Respiratory — gas exchange (lungs, trachea).
  • Skeletal & muscular — support and movement (bones, muscles, joints).

At a joint, muscles work in antagonistic pairs — when one contracts, the other relaxes (e.g. biceps and triceps at the elbow).

flashcards.back: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition.
Controls the cell and contains the DNA / genetic information.
Where respiration happens to release energy.
Controls what substances enter and leave the cell.
Jelly-like fluid where chemical reactions take place.
Contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis.
Made of cellulose; gives the plant cell support and shape.
No nucleus + lots of haemoglobin + biconcave shape to carry more oxygen.
Long projection gives a large surface area to absorb water and minerals.
Long and thin to carry electrical signals over long distances.
Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism.
A group of similar cells working together to do a job.
Different tissues working together (e.g. heart, stomach).
Two muscles that work opposite each other — one contracts as the other relaxes (e.g. biceps/triceps).

commonMistakes.title: Giving plant-only parts to animal cells
commonMistakes.detail: Animal cells do NOT have a cell wall, chloroplasts or a permanent vacuole.

commonMistakes.title: Saying the cell wall controls what enters
commonMistakes.detail: That's the cell membrane. The wall just gives support. Plant cells have BOTH.

commonMistakes.title: Multiplying magnification wrongly
commonMistakes.detail: Total magnification = eyepiece × objective, not added together.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing tissue and organ
commonMistakes.detail: Tissue = similar cells; organ = several different tissues together.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting why a cell is adapted
commonMistakes.detail: Always link the feature to the job, e.g. 'no nucleus SO it can carry more oxygen'.

commonMistakes.title: Saying chlorophyll is a cell part
commonMistakes.detail: Chlorophyll is the green pigment INSIDE chloroplasts; the chloroplast is the structure.

questions.q: What does the nucleus do?
options: Controls the cell and holds DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus controls the cell's activities and contains genetic information (DNA).
questions.hint: It's the control centre.

questions.q: Which part is found ONLY in plant cells?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts (for photosynthesis) are only in plant cells.
questions.hint: Which one is green and makes food?

questions.q: Respiration happens in which cell part?
questions.hint: The 'powerhouse' of the cell.

questions.q: What does the 'N' in MRS GREN stand for?
questions.hint: It's about food.

questions.q: Put these in order from smallest to largest.
questions.explanation: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism.
questions.hint: Start with the building block.

questions.q: A red blood cell has no nucleus so that it can…
questions.explanation: No nucleus leaves more room for haemoglobin to carry oxygen.
questions.hint: More space inside for what gas?

questions.q: Name the cell part that controls what enters and leaves the cell.
questions.hint: Not the wall — the thin layer around the cell.
questions.examMistake: Don't say cell wall — the wall only gives support.

questions.q: State the job of chloroplasts.
questions.modelAnswer: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis to make food (glucose) for the plant.
questions.hint: What green process makes food?
questions.examMistake: Saying 'they make the plant green' is not enough — mention photosynthesis.

questions.q: Which is NOT one of the seven life processes?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is how plants make food, but it is not one of the 7 MRS GREN processes (that's Nutrition).
questions.hint: Think MRS GREN.

questions.q: A microscope eyepiece is ×10 and objective ×20. Total magnification?
questions.hint: Multiply the two lenses.

questions.q: Define the word 'tissue'.
questions.modelAnswer: A tissue is a group of similar cells working together to do a particular job.
questions.hint: Lots of the same kind of cell.
questions.examMistake: Don't confuse it with an organ (which is several tissues).

questions.q: Which structure gives a plant cell its shape and support?
questions.explanation: The cellulose cell wall provides support and shape.
questions.hint: Made of cellulose.

questions.q: Explain two ways a root hair cell is adapted to its function.
questions.modelAnswer: A root hair cell has a long, thin projection that gives it a large surface area to absorb more water and dissolved minerals. It also has a thin cell wall so water can pass in quickly, and many mitochondria to provide energy for active uptake of minerals.
questions.hint: Think about its shape and what it absorbs.
questions.examMistake: Just describing the shape without linking it to absorbing water/minerals.

questions.q: Both animal and plant cells contain which set of parts?
questions.explanation: Nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane and mitochondria are in both.
questions.hint: Which parts are NOT plant-only?

questions.q: Describe the correct order of steps to view a specimen under a microscope.
questions.modelAnswer: Place the slide on the stage. Start with the lowest power objective lens. Use the coarse focus to bring the specimen roughly into focus, then the fine focus for a sharp image. Then switch to a higher power objective and refocus with the fine focus only.
questions.hint: Which power and which focus knob do you start with?
questions.examMistake: Starting on high power makes the specimen hard to find.

questions.q: Why does a muscle cell contain many mitochondria?
options: To release lots of energy for contraction
questions.explanation: Contracting needs energy, supplied by respiration in mitochondria.
questions.hint: What do muscles need to move?

questions.q: Explain how muscles work in antagonistic pairs at the elbow.
questions.modelAnswer: Muscles can only pull, not push, so they work in antagonistic pairs. To bend the arm, the biceps contracts and the triceps relaxes. To straighten the arm, the triceps contracts and the biceps relaxes.
questions.hint: One pulls while the other relaxes.
questions.examMistake: Saying a muscle 'pushes' — muscles only pull.

questions.q: Which sequence correctly describes the digestive system level of organisation?
options: Cell and organ are the same
questions.explanation: Cells form muscle tissue, which (with other tissues) forms the stomach (an organ), part of the digestive system.
questions.hint: Build it up from cells.

questions.q: Why is it important to lower the cover slip at an angle when making a slide?
questions.modelAnswer: Lowering the cover slip slowly at an angle stops air bubbles from being trapped under it, which would block the view and could be mistaken for cell parts.
questions.hint: What might get trapped underneath?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that air bubbles can look like cells.

questions.q: A sperm cell has a tail. This adaptation helps it to…
questions.explanation: The tail (flagellum) lets the sperm swim towards the egg.
questions.hint: How does it reach the egg?

questions.q: State two differences between a palisade leaf cell and a root hair cell, linked to their jobs.
questions.modelAnswer: A palisade cell has many chloroplasts and is near the top of the leaf to absorb light for photosynthesis; a root hair cell has no chloroplasts (it is underground in the dark) but has a long projection for a large surface area to absorb water and minerals.
questions.hint: One captures light, one absorbs water.
questions.examMistake: Giving features without linking them to the job.

questions.q: Which statement about the nucleus is correct?
options: It contains DNA that controls the cell
It is only in plant cells
questions.explanation: The nucleus holds DNA and controls cell activities; it is in both animal and plant cells.
questions.hint: Control centre with genetic info.

questions.q: A student views onion cells under ×100, then changes to the ×400 objective and the image goes blurry and darker. Explain why and what to do.
questions.modelAnswer: At higher magnification you see a smaller area, less light reaches the eye so the image is darker, and the focus changes so it looks blurry. To fix it, refocus using only the fine focus knob and increase the light using the diaphragm/light source. Do not use the coarse focus on high power as it can crack the slide.
questions.hint: Think about light and which focus knob is safe on high power.
questions.examMistake: Using coarse focus on high power risks breaking the slide.

questions.q: Explain fully why a red blood cell is described as a 'specialised cell', referring to three adaptations.
questions.modelAnswer: A specialised cell is adapted in structure to carry out a particular function efficiently. A red blood cell carries oxygen, and it is adapted by: (1) having no nucleus, leaving more room for haemoglobin; (2) being packed with haemoglobin, which binds oxygen; and (3) having a biconcave disc shape, giving a large surface area for oxygen to diffuse across quickly.
questions.hint: Three structural features, each linked to carrying oxygen.
questions.examMistake: Listing features without saying how each helps carry oxygen.

questions.q: An image of a cell measures 50 mm wide under ×500 magnification. What is the cell's real width?
questions.explanation: Real size = image size ÷ magnification = 50 ÷ 500 = 0.1 mm.
questions.hint: Real size = image ÷ magnification.

questions.q: Using the levels of organisation, explain how a single muscle cell relates to the whole circulatory system.
questions.modelAnswer: Many similar muscle cells make up muscle tissue. Muscle tissue, together with other tissues, forms the heart, which is an organ. The heart works with blood vessels (other organs/tissues) to form the circulatory organ system, which is part of the whole organism. So a muscle cell is the smallest building block that ultimately builds the circulatory system.
questions.hint: Build up cell → tissue → organ → system.
questions.examMistake: Skipping levels or calling the heart a tissue.

questions.q: A leaf is green at the top surface but pale underneath. Use your knowledge of cells to explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: The top of the leaf has tightly packed palisade cells containing many chloroplasts to absorb the maximum light for photosynthesis, making it appear dark green. The lower layer (spongy mesophyll) has fewer chloroplasts and air spaces for gas exchange, so it looks paler. Chloroplasts are concentrated where most light is available.
questions.hint: Where are most chloroplasts and why?
questions.examMistake: Not linking chloroplast position to light absorption.

questions.q: Why can a plant cell keep its shape when placed in water, but an animal cell may burst?
options: The strong cellulose cell wall stops the plant cell bursting
questions.explanation: The rigid cell wall resists the pressure as water enters, while an animal cell has only a membrane and can burst.
questions.hint: What extra strong layer do plant cells have?

questions.q: Explain why scientists use a stain such as iodine when looking at cells under the microscope.
questions.modelAnswer: Cells are mostly colourless and transparent, so their parts are hard to see. A stain such as iodine is absorbed by certain structures (e.g. it stains starch and makes the nucleus and other parts darker), increasing the contrast so the cell parts can be seen clearly.
questions.hint: Cells are see-through — what does colour add?
questions.examMistake: Saying the stain 'feeds' the cell — it only adds contrast.

questions.q: A cell has a cell wall, a nucleus, a vacuole and chloroplasts. State whether it is a plant or animal cell and justify using TWO features.
questions.modelAnswer: It is a plant cell. It has a cellulose cell wall, which animal cells do not have, and it has chloroplasts for photosynthesis, which are only found in plant cells. (The large permanent vacuole also supports this.)
questions.hint: Which features are plant-only?
questions.examMistake: Naming features that are in both cells (like nucleus) as evidence.
